title: The Afternoon Brief: Iran strikes, oil past $100, and a Saudi deal with strings attached
dek: The US and Iran remain locked in an 11th and 12th night of strikes, oil has broken $100 a barrel for the first time since May, and a Saudi nuclear agreement arrives with conditions still being worked out.
author: GoCheckMyNews
key_fact: Several of the day's biggest stories, from the Saudi nuclear deal's enrichment terms to Nicaragua's election ban to Trump's stated nuclear-site target, are still contingent or unconfirmed rather than settled facts.
bottom_line: The day's dominant story is escalation across the Middle East: 11 and then 12 consecutive nights of US strikes on Iran, a Trump statement floating a future strike on an underground nuclear site near Natanz, Houthi attacks on Saudi tankers, and Brent crude crossing $100 a barrel for the first time since May. Alongside that, a US-Saudi nuclear cooperation agreement was signed with its enrichment terms and an Israel-recognition condition from Trump still unresolved, and Ukraine replaced both its military commander-in-chief and defense minister amid the war. Other developments, France's under-15 social media ban, Nicaragua's planned vote to bar opposition parties, a fraud case against former Southern Water executives, an EU fine against Google, and early pancreatic cancer vaccine trial results, proceeded on their own tracks. The through-line is that several of today's biggest stories are stated intentions or conditional agreements rather than settled outcomes. Coming checkpoints include any US action against the Natanz-area site, Saudi Arabia's response to the Abraham Accords condition, Nicaragua's early-August vote, and Google's 60-day compliance window on its EU fine.
body: The thread running through Wednesday and Thursday is escalation without resolution: a Middle East conflict that has widened from Iran to Red Sea shipping to oil markets, a Ukrainian military shake-up mid-war, and a Saudi nuclear agreement whose actual terms remain unsettled even as it is announced.

The US struck Iranian targets for an 11th and then 12th consecutive night, according to US Central Command, hitting military operations centers, maritime capabilities, aircraft hangars, and logistics infrastructure, with explosions reported in Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, and Bushehr. President Trump said on Truth Social and in public remarks that the next target could be Pickaxe Mountain, an underground site near Natanz suspected of housing an undeclared enrichment facility, saying "we'll be hitting that area pretty soon, and very heavily." This is a stated threat and public posture from Trump, not a confirmed order or timeline for a strike; Iran's military command has warned it would retaliate against any attack on its nuclear facilities. Separately, Yemen's Houthi movement claimed missile and drone attacks on two Saudi-flagged tankers, the Encelia and Layla, in the Red Sea. Saudi authorities confirmed the Encelia was struck and caught fire at the bow, with all crew safe; the Houthis said the attacks were retaliation for what they described as violations of a blockade they announced Monday. No confirmation has emerged of a strike on the Layla.

Oil markets registered the strain directly: Brent crude rose more than 6% to cross $100 a barrel for the first time since May, according to BBC News, driven by the Houthi attacks and the US strikes on Iran together. UK petrol has risen 5p per liter since July 1 to nearly £1.56, and US gasoline has topped $4 a gallon, up from $3.92 a month earlier, per AAA data cited by the BBC.

Ukraine's military leadership changed hands amid the war: President Zelensky removed Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi as commander-in-chief and appointed Maj Gen Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, the third such change since Russia's 2022 invasion. The move followed days of street protests and came after Zelensky had also removed Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov, 35, replacing him with Yevhenii Khmara, a former Security Service official. Drapatyi is described in reporting as a close friend of Fedorov's.

The US and Saudi Arabia signed a civil nuclear cooperation agreement that the US Energy Department said provides "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy program," alongside a bilateral safeguards agreement. Reporting says the deal could eventually allow Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium domestically at a US-built facility, a capability the US has not previously granted another country, but the full text has not been published and the terms of any enrichment limits remain unclear pending a joint US-Saudi feasibility study. Trump separately posted that the deal "will be approved, but is totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining" the Abraham Accords and recognizing Israel, and said the agreement involves no enrichment and pertains only to non-military use, a statement in some tension with reporting on the enrichment provisions. The White House said Trump had discussed the Israel-recognition condition with Saudi leaders "on numerous previous occasions," but it is not established whether Saudi Arabia has agreed to that condition. The deal goes to Congress for review.

Elsewhere, France's parliament adopted a law banning social media for under-15s, the first such enforcement in Europe: from September 2026 people under 15 cannot open new accounts, and from January 2027 age verification will be required across all existing accounts in France, meaning current users will need to verify their age to keep using the platforms. Nicaragua's National Assembly plans to vote on legislation to bar opposition parties from elections, with reporting describing the vote as planned for the first week of August, following President Ortega's July 20 statement that "there will be no more elections for them to try to seize the government." A phase 1 trial of an experimental pancreatic cancer vaccine, mKRAS-VAX, produced KRAS-targeted immune responses in 18 of 20 high-risk participants at Johns Hopkins, with no participants developing cancer over about 16.5 months of follow-up; study authors cautioned that larger trials are needed to confirm whether observed cyst shrinkage was caused by the vaccine or occurred spontaneously. In the UK, a High Court ruling cleared the way for a fraud trial against former Southern Water CEO Matthew Wright and three ex-officers, accused of manipulating water quality tests between 2012 and 2017 to avoid roughly £45 million in penalties. The EU fined Google €890 million under the Digital Markets Act, split between €460 million for favoring its own travel services in search and €430 million over Play Store pricing rules. Tropical Storm Bertha made landfall in Louisiana and moved toward Texas with 45 mph winds, expected to weaken and dissipate inland by Friday.

Why these events are converging is not fully explained in the reporting. The trigger for the collapse of the earlier US-Iran ceasefire is not specified in official statements, nor is what would restart diplomacy. The reporting does not establish why Zelensky moved on his military and defense leadership in this specific sequence beyond the protests and public pressure described.
No desk boards were available this cycle to check the day's figures against an official record.

The coming days carry several concrete checkpoints: whether the US follows through on any strike near Natanz and how Iran responds; whether Oman's mediation affects the Houthi-Saudi standoff in the Red Sea; Saudi Arabia's response to Trump's Abraham Accords condition and the deal's path through Congress; Drapatyi's early operational decisions in Ukraine; Nicaragua's early-August legislative vote; Google's compliance decision within its 60-day window on the EU fine; and further trial data on the pancreatic cancer vaccine.
